coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical-mechanical coupling measurements
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yueming
Bhargava, Bhuvsmita
Stewart, David M.
Talin, A. Alec
Rubloff, Gary W.
Albertus, Paul
coredata.dc:description: Lithium metal solid-state batteries (LiSSBs) present new challenges in the measurement of material, component, and cell mechanical behaviors and in the measurement and theory of fundamental mechanical-electrochemical (thermodynamics, transport, and kinetics) couplings. Here, we classify the major mechanical and electrochemical-mechanical (ECM) studies underway and provide an overview of major mechanical testing platforms. We emphasize key distinctions among testing platforms, including tip- vs. platen-based sample compression, surface- vs. volume-based analysis, ease of integration with a vacuum or inert atmosphere environment, the ability to control and measure force/displacement over long periods of time, ranges of force and contact area, and others. Among the techniques we review, nanoindentation platforms offer some unique benefits associated with being able to use both tip-based nanoindentation techniques as well as platen-based compression over areas approaching 1 mm2. Sample design is also important: while most efforts are particle-based (i.e., using particles of solid electrolyte and cathode-active materials and densifying them using sintering or pressure), the resulting electrochemical response is from the overall collection of particles present. In contrast, thin-film (<1 μm) solid-state battery materials (e.g., Li, LiPON, LCO) provide well defined and uniform structures well suited for fundamental electrochemical-mechanical studies and offer an important opportunity to drive underlying scientific advances in LiSSB and other areas. We believe there are exciting opportunities to advance the measurement of both mechanical properties and electrochemical-mechanical couplings through the careful and novel co-design of test structures and experimental approaches for LiSSB materials, components, and cells.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium metal solid-state batteries (LiSSBs) present new challenges in the measurement of material, component, and cell mechanical behaviors and in the measurement and theory of fundamental mechanical-electrochemical (thermodynamics, transport, and kinetics) couplings. Here, we classify the major mechanical and electrochemical-mechanical (ECM) studies underway and provide an overview of major mechanical testing platforms. We emphasize key distinctions among testing platforms, including tip- vs. platen-based sample compression, surface- vs. volume-based analysis, ease of integration with a vacuum or inert atmosphere environment, the ability to control and measure force/displacement over long periods of time, ranges of force and contact area, and others. Among the techniques we review, nanoindentation platforms offer some unique benefits associated with being able to use both tip-based nanoindentation techniques as well as platen-based compression over areas approaching 1 mm2. Sample design is also important: while most efforts are particle-based (i.e., using particles of solid electrolyte and cathode-active materials and densifying them using sintering or pressure), the resulting electrochemical response is from the overall collection of particles present. In contrast, thin-film (<1 μm) solid-state battery materials (e.g., Li, LiPON, LCO) provide well defined and uniform structures well suited for fundamental electrochemical-mechanical studies and offer an important opportunity to drive underlying scientific advances in LiSSB and other areas. We believe there are exciting opportunities to advance the measurement of both mechanical properties and electrochemical-mechanical couplings through the careful and novel co-design of test structures and experimental approaches for LiSSB materials, components, and cells.

Li-ion battery production is being rapidly scaled up, but opportunities for technical improvements in key metrics such as energy content per mass and volume and cell-level safety remain. Replacing liquid with solid-state electrolytes, and using high-capacity Li metal or Si anode materials, is a principal focus for current R&D investment. However, shifting to a fully (or nearly fully) solid-state cell with a high-volume-change anode such as Li or Si introduces mechanical stresses and strains absent from current Li-ion technology. Important work on measuring the mechanical properties of solid-state battery materials (e.g., Li metal, sulfide solid electrolytes) has taken place and is currently underway. There has also been work on measurement of the fundamental electrochemical-mechanical couplings in the areas of thermodynamics, transport, and kinetics.

However, significant measurement opportunities continue to exist in both the area of mechanical properties and electrochemical-mechanical coupling. In addition, the specific co-design of measurement sample and experimental platform is a promising direction to advance the measurements in these areas. Areas of particular importance include the mechanical properties of Li metal and other high-volume change electrode materials at submicron to tens of micron length scales with mechanical boundary conditions relevant to practical cells (especially the material plastic mechanical properties), and the measurement of the coupling of thermodynamic, kinetic, and transport properties with mechanical stress across a wide range of lithiation states of electrode materials. Advances in our knowledge of mechanical properties and electrochemical-mechanical coupling will enable a more precise quantitative understanding of solid-state battery performance and degradation, and the most promising materials and cell architectures.

Solid-state batteries offer unique measurement challenges in the areas of mechanical properties and electrochemical-mechanical coupling. Here, we analyze the leading measurement approaches for the mechanical properties of solid-state battery materials and structures, fundamental studies of electrochemical (including thermodynamics, transport, and kinetics)-mechanical coupling, applied studies of solid-state batteries under mechanical load, and the range of samples used in these studies. We emphasize the importance of co-design of test structures and measurement platforms for both fundamental and applied advances.

ce:section.ce:para:
Li-ion batteries have undergone great commercial development during the past two decades due to higher specific energy (>250 Wh/kg) and cycle life (1,000 or more) than alternative chemistries, leading to their widespread use in portable electronics, electric vehicles, and other applications. 1
,
2
,
3
,
4 However, cells with higher specific energy, greater cell-level safety, and other improvements could advance numerous applications from electric aircraft to implantable biomedical devices. 5
,
6 In particular, shifting to a lithium metal anode could substantially increase specific energy and energy density by >30%, but the use of lithium metal with a liquid electrolyte has been challenging due to an unstable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) 7 resulting from chemical reactivity and morphological upheaval during cycling, especially at charge rates of commercial interest (e.g., C/2 or higher). 8

A solid-state electrolyte may mitigate these two problems by providing a more chemically stable reaction interface as well as mechanically suppressing nonuniform volume changes near the interface. 9
,
10 However, the shift to solid electrolytes (SEs) results in evolving solid-solid interfaces as the cell is cycled that require interdisciplinary studies to address coupled electrochemical and mechanical (ECM) behaviors, including how those behaviors relate to sample or battery fabrication, performance, and degradation. While the majority of work in this field is focused on answering how uniaxial applied stress affects the performance of small battery cells (e.g., with 1 cm2 solid electrolyte separators), there are also some studies that address the fundamental scientific ECM coupling questions that arise from nanometer up to millimeter scales.

Figure 1 illustrates a cell structure with Li metal, a solid electrolyte composed of densified particles with various grain orientations (e.g., doped or undoped Li7La3Zr2O12 [LLZO]), and a porous cathode (e.g., a NMC811 cathode with a sulfide solid electrolyte). Many ECM studies focus on the impacts of incomplete contact between solid-solid interfaces, which affects the uniformity of electrochemical reactions in both the anode (affecting Li metal stripping and plating) and the cathode (resulting in active material particle isolation). The effects of incomplete and potentially time-dependent solid-solid interface contact ultimately manifests in the measured current-voltage behavior. The interfacial adhesion between active materials and the SE may also affect the evolution of the solid-solid interface contact. 11
,
12

Of particular interest for many studies are reversible and irreversible processes at the Li metal/SE interface, where current (shown in blue) and stress (shown in orange) concentration is often identified as important to the plating and stripping processes. 13 The mechanical properties of Li metal—which is ductile and undergoes significant plastic and creep deformation at practical battery conditions—and the impact of the applied stack pressure on this interface are frequently studied as well. 14 Other ECM studies focus on the composite cathode, where volumetric changes in the active material create changes in the stress and strain states. Stack pressure is widely observed to play a key role in reducing the cell impedance and improving the cycle life of solid-state cathodes assembled from inorganic solid electrolyte particles, although significant variations in local stress state within a composite cathode under uniaxial load is expected. 15

Figure 1 also includes several of the important couplings between mechanical state and electrochemical behavior, including in the areas of thermodynamics, kinetics, and transport. On the Li metal side, the stress state can impact the self-diffusivity within Li metal (
σ ↔
D
e f f e c t i v e
), as well as both the thermodynamics (
σ ↔ U
) and kinetics (
σ ↔
i 0
) of lithium plating and stripping. The mechanical state at the Li metal interface can also affect the rate and extent of interfacial reactions, including due to the influence of mechanics on morphology evolution at the Li metal interface. Within the solid eletrolyte itself, the stress and strain states can impact the ionic conductivity (
σ , ε ↔
κ
i o n i c
). 16 As for the cathode region, the mechanical state (either influenced by external mechanical load or cycling induced volumetric changes) can have a strong impact on the lithium concentration distribution (
σ , ε ↔
c
L i
), 17 that further introduces localized stresses upon cycling and may result in cathode/SE delamination and a deteriorated electron conduction pathway (
σ , ε ↔
R int
). The work of Kerman et al. includes a useful classification of the mechanisms shown in Figure 1 and how they affect electrochemical behaviors. 18

ECM coupling is important at each stage of a solid-state battery’s fabrication and use, and the ultimate goal for applied ECM studies is to understand and overcome the challenges at each of these stages.
(1)
Fabrication of particle-based battery stacks typically results in non-conformal contact (i.e., load is carried by a few points of contact with limited contact area) among particles and between adjacent cell layers (e.g., Li metal foil or composite electrodes) and thus nonuniform current flow.
(2)
Operation results in volume changes at both electrodes as the Li cycles, and thereby dynamic stresses and strains within the cell structure. The stress states affect the thermodynamics, transport, and kinetics within the cell, and thereby the electrochemical performance of the cell.
(3)
Degradation occurs through numerous ECM coupling mechanisms, including void and dendrite formation at Li metal/solid separator interfaces, and particle fracture and delamination within cathodes.

The processes described in Figure 1 and throughout this introduction have been described in a range of experimental and modeling studies, but the focus of this article is on the experimental methods used to study ECM coupling in both practical cells as well for fundamental understanding. Our emphasis is on solid-state batteries with a Li metal anode. The structure for the remainder of this paper is to first classify and describe three main types of ECM coupling experimental studies, including motivations and limitations, and then to describe the samples, specific instruments, and remaining gaps for each experimental platform. Readers particularly interested in the experimental platforms currently used may skip directly to that section. We conclude with an outlook on future opportunities for this field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Classification and description of the main types of electrochemical–mechanical coupling experimental studies
ce:section.ce:para:
Our classification of ECM coupling experimental studies, and specific examples from prominent studies, are presented in Figure 2 . The three main branches of study are illustrated in Figure 2A. Note that category (2), material mechanical properties, does not technically involve the simultaneous application of mechanical and electrochemical test methods, but because the mechanical testing is very closely linked to electrochemical processes and properties, we have listed it in the content of ECM coupling in our classification.
(1)
Cell performance: this typically consists of testing single-layer cells with an area of ∼1 cm2 in a compression fixture with a uniaxial load. Rate and cycling tests are carried out, and both common battery metrics (e.g., Ragone plots, capacity fading, short circuiting) and results unique to solid-state batteries (e.g., critical current densities for plating and stripping of Li metal) are obtained. This is a well-established type of ECM study, with experimental tools easily accessible to academia and industry, and with clear practical significance for possible commercial cells.
(2)
Material mechanical properties: measuring the mechanical properties (elastic-plastic, creep, volume change, fracture, etc.) of the materials used in LiSSB is crucial for the quantitative understanding and prediction of stress and strain distributions. The relevant mechanical properties are often a strong function of lithium content (which is typically controlled with electrochemical methods), temperature, size, shape, and mechanical loading. A range of experimental approaches and sample types have been used to measure the material mechanical properties.
(3)
Thermodynamics, kinetics, and transport: measuring fundamental ECM coupling behaviors in the areas of thermodynamics, transport, and kinetics are of both basic scientific significance, as well as important for modeling of ECM coupling in practical cells. Compared with topics (1) and (2), this topic has many fewer experimental contributions, in part because of challenging and exacting experimental design requirements. This often require samples of highly uniform composition and mechanical properties that are best achieved with vapor deposition techniques (chemical vapor deposition (CVD), atomic layer deposition (ALD), or sputtering) which, while not uncommon for battery materials, are significantly less accessible than particle-based techniques (e.g., preparation of particle-based slurries, followed by casting then sintering or cold pressing).

In addition to these three major classes of ECM coupling studies shown in the outer ring of Figure 2A, the inner rings (in dark blue) also show current experimental platforms used in each area as well as (in gray and dark green) example ECM couplings. The gray regions contain letters (B)–(G) that correspond to Figures 2B–2G These additional panels show specific examples of experimental coupling studies listed in the gray regions. In the remainder of this section, we provide additional description of each of the three classes of ECM coupling experimental studies shown in Figure 2, with a particular focus on the motivation and limitations within each of these areas.

The motivation of cell performance-ECM coupling studies is to improve the cell characteristics by applying a stack pressure, ideally one small enough to be compatible with commercial use (which is <∼1 MPa for electric vehicles). Indeed, stack pressure is recognized as a new and critical variable for battery studies, as the magnitude of the stack pressure that LiSSBs can sustain is much greater than for liquid electrolyte Li-ion cells (even 100s of MPa is commonly reported in laboratory studies). A stack pressure experiment involves placing an anode/electrolyte/cathode cell stack inside a load frame, applying a uniaxial compressive force, and measuring the cell performance and degradation during cycling (see Figure 4). Numerous phenomena may be explored with this arrangement, such as the influence of applied stress on cycle life (Figure 2B), critical current density for plating and stripping, cell resistance, and others. 14
,
15
,
24

The applied stress can be either beneficial or detrimental, depending on its magnitude and the materials present. For example, in one study LiNixMnyCozO2 (NMC)-based composite cathodes showed reduced discharge capacity at a 5 MPa stack pressure. 25 In a different study, however, under a higher stack pressure of 25 MPa, a LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2/ Li10SnP2S12/ Li4Ti5O12 LiSSB delivered an improved maximum discharge capacity of 187 mAhg−1. 26 As another example, for bulk Si anodes, at the very high pressure of 230 MPa, the initial discharge capacity of Si electrodes decreased by ∼ 50% compared with a baseline test done at 3 MPa. However, this high pressure also stabilized the charge capacity making the Si electrodes reversible over >20 cycles reported in this study. 27

Similar studies have been done for electrolytes as well; for glass ceramics (Li7P3S11) and sulfide-based (Li2S-P2S5) electrolytes, a stack pressure up to 50 MPa mainly increases ionic conductivity by improving compaction of the of solid electrolyte pellet. In another example, for a microcrystalline electrolyte (Li6PS5Br), a stack pressure >200 MPa closed the interparticle distance and improved contact even after the fabrication pressure was released. 28

Finally, void formation at a Li metal anode during stripping can be suppressed with applied stress because high pressures allow Li to sufficiently creep and conform to the rough electrode/electrolyte interface. For example, for a symmetric Li/ Li6PS5Cl/ Li cell, the critical current density for void formation increases from 0.75 mA/cm2 at 1 MPa to 2.5 mA/cm2 at 5 MPa applied stack pressure at 80°C. 29 The half-cell impedance of the Li/ Li6PS5Cl/Li symmetric cell at 25°C was reported to decrease from 500 Ω at 1 MPa stack pressure, to 110 Ω at 5 MPa, and to 32 Ω at 25 MPa. 14

While a uniaxial stress test has great practical relevance and is straightforward to implement, a principal limitation is that within a few μm of the interface where electrochemical reactions take place, the applied stress is expected to vary significantly based on surface contact properties 30
,
31
,
32 and local material defects as well as composition variations. Thus, the measured
i − V
behavior of the cell arises from a distribution of mechanical (e.g., contact properties) and electrochemical (e.g., nonuniform interfacial reaction rate) conditions. Hence, while a solid-state battery stack with a uniaxial load can provide engineering insights into battery structure design and optimization, it is not suitable for fundamental, accurate, and quantitative studies of the interplay between stress/strain and electrochemical behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material mechanical behavior: Motivations and limitations
ce:section.ce:para:
Understanding and quantifying the root causes of mechanical effects on cell performance, and the development of accurate ECM coupling models, requires accurate measurements and descriptions of the mechanical constitutive behavior at a range of length scales and loadings. Hence, the principal motivations of mechanical testing on battery materials are to fill property measurement gaps, which is often challenging because of the following:
(1)
Batteries consist of materials with a wide range of mechanical responses (i.e., elastic, plastic and creep), some of which require specialized and involved measurement techniques that combine electrochemical and mechanical functionalities.
(2)
Batteries are dynamic with changing local electrode and interface compositions, which in turn affects the mechanical properties.
(3)
The mechanical properties of some battery materials are a function of length scale, mechanical load, and temperature.

Solid-state battery materials include, (1) brittle ceramics (such as metal oxide cathodes, with moduli
E > 50
G P a
) that typically undergo <∼0.5% elastic deformation followed by sudden fracture, (2) intermediate materials with moduli between oxide ceramic and organic polymers (such as sulfide-based solid electrolytes 9
,
33 ), which can be compacted with high pressures at room temperature and have important plastic and rate-dependent deformations, 34 and (3) compliant materials such as lithium metal (
E < 10
G P a
), which yields at <1 MPa in bulk tension tests and has significant creep and other rate-dependent deformation behaviors under battery-relevant conditions.

In addition to mechanical material properties, the solid-solid contact mechanics among battery materials can be complicated by large differences in the mechanical properties of the two materials forming an interface. For example, measuring the contact mechanics of voids and dendrites during lithium stripping and plating on a stiff ceramic solid electrolyte is more challenging than measuring the hard contact between two brittle solids. Measuring the interfacial properties that quantify the adhesion at a solid-solid interface, including between grains, remains both important and challenging.

The changes in lithium content due to cycling may lead to two mechanical issues within an electrode. First, the volume changes that take place within active materials over expected ranges of Li composition should be measured, as these volume changes may cause stresses of several GPa. 35
,
36
,
37 Common transition metal oxide cathode materials have a few percent volume change over their practical range of Li content, and LiFePO4 has a theoretical volume change of ∼7% during its charge to FePO4. 38 Several of the conversion cathodes of interest have significantly greater volume change (e.g., FeS2 has a volume change of ∼159% with four Li per Fe, FeF3 has a volume change of ∼15% with three Li per Fe). 39
,
40 In terms of anode materials, graphite has a volume change of 13%, while the volume change for fully lithiated Si (Li4.4Si) is over 300%. 41
,
42

Second, conversion and alloying-type anodes (e.g., Si as shown in Figure 2E) and cathodes (e.g., transition metal fluorides and sulfides) may undergo substantial phase changes as a function of lithium content, which can substantially change their elastic moduli and other mechanical properties. While many solid electrolytes are single ion conductors without bulk compositional changes during cycling, some authors have proposed that space-charge formation can result in Li+ concentration gradients near an electrode interface and affect fracture. 43
,
44 Even for a seemingly well-studied material such as Si, only a few studies have been focused on the variation of its mechanical properties as a function of Li content, temperature, and size. 45
,
46

The mechanical responses of battery materials have also been observed to depend on material size, shape, microstructure, and temperature as well as mechanical loading and boundary conditions. For cathode-active materials (e.g., NMC), the anisotropy in Li conduction planes, size, and shape of primary particles resulting in different charging rates has been observed to influences the modulus, hardness, and tensile strength obtained by nanoindentation due to microcracking along grain boundaries. 47 To take lithium metal as an example, the uniaxial and compressive mechanical behavior of bulk lithium was recently measured carefully, 48 but measurements on Li metal films with the thickness and aspect ratio expected for commercial cells (i.e., width:thickness aspect ratios of >1,000:1) are not yet available. The elastic and plastic properties of Li metal also show strong length scale dependence as assessed with micropillar compression and nanoindentation studies. 49
,
50 However, such studies are few, and mostly based on commercially purchased foils that have some degree of surface contamination and bulk defects rather than electrochemically plated lithium, which may have higher purity and/or different impurities than commercial foils. We emphasize the critical need for novel experimental designs and work on the mechanical and other properties of electrochemically plated lithium, which is likely to have higher bulk and interface purity than the foils or vapor deposited lithium primarily used today. This need is strengthened by the industrial relevance of cells in which the Li anode is exclusively formed from the Li removed from the cathode on the first charge. 51
,
52

While there are some groups doing detailed mechanical property measurements of LiSSB materials that are significantly advancing this field, there are several factors that make such measurements challenging. One is the high chemical reactivity of many battery materials with air and/or moisture, requiring measurements to be done in an inert atmosphere. Keeping test samples from impurities and side reactions can be challenging when the lithiation state is changed electrochemically, which typically takes hours to reach equilibrium. Moving a test sample between electrochemical instruments and mechanical testers can result in air and moisture exposure that affects the sample interface and bulk composition. It is also challenging to place materials in configurations with mechanical boundary conditions and loadings that directly mimic what occurs within a solid-state cell. For example, the properties obtained from a micropillar compression test (e.g., a 1-μm-diameter pillar with free cylindrical walls) are not directly relevant to the mechanical behavior of a lithium foil with a 1,000:1 width:thickness aspect ratio under a uniaxial stress due to the far different mechanical boundaries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamics, transport, and kinetics: Motivations and limitations
ce:section.ce:para: Changes in mechanical stress and strain can directly alter the thermodynamic states of reactants and products, the kinetics of interfacial charge transfer, and the rate of ionic conduction and diffusion, providing a clear motivation to measure and understand these couplings. The larger stresses present in solid-state structures vs. those with liquid electrolytes require measurement of these fundamental couplings to values of 100s of MPa and above, and both elastic and plastic strains. In this section, we discuss motivation and context for thermodynamic, transport, and kinetic coupling with mechanics in that order.

First, for mechanic-thermodynamic coupling, the change in mechanical stress of a material will change its thermodynamic state, just as pressure-volume work in a fluid system changes its thermodynamic state. This is of clear importance to the direction and rate of reaction, for example, in models of current redistribution away from highly stressed Li metal at a dendrite tip in predictions of solid electrolyte fracture. 53 Stresses of 100s of MPa may be present in Li metal at a dendrite tip, and even higher stresses of 10s of GPa have been predicted in cathode particles. Shifts in the current distribution, which arise when the equilibrium potential shifts under stress, for Li metal directly affect the location of plating (and hence dendrite initiation/growth) and stripping (and hence void initiation/growth), while for an insertion or conversion active material particle the current distribution affects the location of (de)lithiation, which in turn couples to the location of volume change and stress distribution in the material. 54
,
55

The magnitude of the stress-potential coupling is proportional to the molar volume (for a pure phase such as Li) or partial molar volume (for a variable composition phase, such as LiyCoO2) and the stress state (i.e.,
Δ U =
V ̲
L i
F
Δ p
, where
U
is the equilibrium potential,
V ̲
L i
is the molar or partial molar volume of Li,
F
is Faraday’s constant, and
p
is the pressure). 19 This effect of stress on the equilibrium potential as a function of concentration has been derived using solution thermodynamics and can be measured directly. 56
,
57 To provide context for the magnitude of this coupling, for Li metal the stress-potential coupling is 0.125 mV/MPa, for Li in In it is 0.122 mV/MPa, and for Li0.8CoO2 cathode it is 0.102 mV/MPa. 58
,
59

Second, for mechanics-transport coupling, the state of stress and strain in a material can impact the transport of species within that material, including Li (in the case of an active material) and Li+ (in the case of an ionic conductor). For example, for Li+ conductivity, a 2020 study by Qi et al. 59 showed through molecular dynamics simulations that cracking and lithium propagation in the solid electrolyte can be inhibited by introducing compressive hydrostatic stresses (10 GPa) in the solid electrolyte, without significantly reducing its ionic conductivity. The effect of stress on Li transport through inorganic solid electrolytes depends on the Young’s moduli and their Li-ion conductivities. For high modulus oxide solid electrolytes such as LLZO, the impact of localized stresses on Li diffusion was insignificant. However, in an ab initio molecular dynamics study on the ionic conductivity of low-moduli sulfide electrolyte such as Li10GeP2S12, a 5-fold increase in ionic conductivity was calculated with a 2% tensile strain, and a two orders of magnitude reduction was calculated under a compressive strain of 2%. 60

There are few direct experimental measurements of stress-transport coupling for Li+ conductors. One experimental study by Yamada et al. 61 used spark plasma sintering to generate residual tensile stresses of up to 100 MPa, and for this stress range, the authors found that while ionic conductivity did not change in the Li6.5La3Zr1.5Ta0.5O12 bulk, the grain boundary resistance increased by 25% as pressure increased from 10 to 50 MPa. This dependence of grain boundary resistance on residual stresses could have important implications in the overall battery performance.

In addition to ionic transport, mechanical stress can substantially affect the electronic conductivity of the electrode, as was recently demonstrated by Fuller et al. for LiCoO2 (LCO). 17 This coupling arises because Li (or Li vacancies) effectively act as electronic dopants in most insertion oxides. Since stress can cause Li motion within the electrodes, it follows that it also can affect the local electronic conductivity. While this coupling is potentially less important in liquid electrolyte cells, it could lead to substantial variability in charging rates in LiSSBs, where conductive additive may not be as uniformly distributed.

Third, changes in the mechanical state of an active material and/or electrolyte may affect the reaction transition state energy barriers, and hence the interfacial kinetics. Several experimental studies measure interfacial charge transfer resistance as a function of applied stress at the interface. 62
,
63
,
64 One such electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement done on a silicon composite electrode revealed that under a compression loading of 675 kPa, the charge transfer resistance increased by 14.8% and decreased by 9.6% in a tensile loading of 675 kPa. 65 However, measurements of stress-interfacial kinetic couplings generally struggle to separate the effect of stress on the activation energy barrier from the effects of solid-solid contact.

The challenges associated with measurements of material mechanical behavior and cell performance apply to the measurement of thermodynamic, transport, and kinetics as well: electrode inhomogeneities, nonuniformities of applied stress, property dependence on Li content, and reactivity of the materials all challenge the design and interpretation of measurements of thermodynamics, transport, and kinetic behaviors. We also note that for applied and/or local stresses of <∼10 MPa, large changes in cell performance, the plastic deformation of Li metal, interfacial contact (and hence measured interfacial charge transfer resistance), etc. may be observed, while stresses in the 10s to 100s of MPa (and higher) may be required to affect bulk properties such as ionic conductivity, thermodynamic potential, or true interfacial properties such as transition state heights for charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Existing sample design, measurement platforms, and remaining measurement system gaps
ce:section.ce:para: In this section we focus on two major topics related to current methods for carrying out the three major classes of ECM measurements presented in the previous section: (1) design of test structures and (2) principal measurement platforms. In Figure 3 , we present the four major types of samples that have been used in ECM studies (particle-based vs. thin film, and stacked vs. lateral), while in Figure 4 , we present the principal measurement platforms in use today.

The most prevalent sample design for ECM coupling measurements is a stacked cell with particle-based electrodes and separator; this structure has the most industrial relevance and is straightforward to assemble in a lab. Figure 3A illustrates a particle-based, stacked LiSSB structure with area ∼1 cm2 under uniform applied uniaxial stress. As shown in the image, there are several items of note about this sample type. First, due to the particle-based fabrication process, the electrode/SE interface is rough and reflects the particle sizes used (typically submicron to 10s of microns) and the preparation method. Incomplete solid-solid contact at varying angles between particles and layers leads to a nonuniform stress distribution despite the use of a uniform applied load, and the stress distribution contributes to nonuniform electrochemical behavior. Second, a stacked structure with all cell layers under the applied load results in both electrode/electrolyte interfaces under the same stress, which makes analysis of ECM coupling behavior at a single interface difficult or impossible. To avoid these challenges, alternative samples have been designed (1) to improve material uniformity and interfacial contact such that the mechanical states within the structure are uniform and known, and (2) to mechanically isolate a single electrode/electrolyte interface or cell material to identify its role in an ECM coupling response.

Thin film structures (e.g., submicron layers deposited through a vapor process such as CVD, ALD, sputtering, or thermal evaporation) shown in Figures 3C and 3D are well positioned to produce the more uniform stress fields desired for fundamental ECM coupling studies. Thin film deposition can leverage decades of experience in patterning and provide test structures with precisely controlled geometric features, as shown in previous work on thin film structures on Si wafers. 68
,
69 There are a number of well-established methods for depositing thin film battery materials such as LixPOyNz (LiPON), LLZO, Li, Na, and Mg metal, LCO, Si, LiV2O5 (LVO), Li4Ti5O12 (LTO), SnO2, MnO2, FeF3, FeSx, etc, 68
,
69
,
70
,
71 and also for applying mechanical forces and measuring the mechanical properties of thin film structures. Working with thin film structures also opens up the possibility of using patterning and nanostructures to isolate interfaces and to create well-defined nonuniformities. Further interaction between the microfabrication and battery communities could advance the creation of novel and improved thin film structures with unique advantages for ECM coupling studies.

In addition to the physical form of the sample, the arrangement of the electrodes and separator can also be varied. In particular, Figures 3B and 3D show lateral cell designs in which the two electrodes are placed on the same side of the electrolyte. 19 With a lateral electrode structure, a single electrode/electrolyte interface can be isolated mechanically, and a mechanical force can also be applied to only the electrolyte region between the electrodes, or other configurations. For thin-film-based lateral cells, the electrodes need to be far enough apart to achieve mechanical decoupling, but close enough to keep the cell resistance within the instrument tolerances. One such lateral structure is shown in Figure 3D, where a LVO cathode and Si anode is deposited on a Si substrate, enveloped by LiPON solid electrolyte.

Next, we discuss the major mechanical testing platforms in terms of key attributes important for carrying out ECM studies. We present these attributes, along with schematics of major testing platforms, in Figure 4. Figure 4A shows how the area of contact between the mechanical tester and the sample ranges by a factor of 1014, from approaching 1 nm × 1 nm for atomic force microscopy (AFM), to 1 cm × 1 cm for load frames and universal testers. The range of applied pressure varies from kPa to GPa levels. In addition, while a typical universal tester may have a displacement resolution in the 1–10 μm range, the other platforms may achieve displacement resolution of ∼1 nm or less in practical conditions. Another key point in Figure 4A is that some platforms apply or generate a stress uniformly over the sample area, while some platforms make use of indentation, which inherently introduces a nonuniform stress field beneath the indenter tip. Because electrochemistry happens at an interface, and for fundamental studies of ECM coupling it is important to have a well-defined and uniform stress within the specimen volume, there are distinct opportunities offered by instruments that can apply controlled uniform pressure over a well-defined sample area for days or longer.

Figures 4B–4E show major platforms used to date. Figure 4B is a lab-made screw platform for measuring system performance. The multibeam optical sensor (MOS) method shown in Figure 4C involves measuring strain of the topside of a wafer, allowing calculation of stress in films deposited on the wafer bottom side. Note that we have not included this platform in Figure 4A because it does not involve the independent application of a defined force. AFM shown in Figure 4D can be configured in multiple ways, including conductive AFM, electrochemical AFM, and AFM indentation. Finally, Figure 4E shows a lateral cell construction in which electrochemical measurements are performed while a single separator/electrode interface is under uniform pressure applied by universal mechanical tester (UMT) or a nanoindenter with a flat platen.

It is important to note that a significant gap exists between the two most often used categories of experiments, namely the AFM/nanoindenter category and the screw tester/UMT category. This gap in the meso-length scale (μm–mm) and low force (μN–N) range can be filled with a nanoindenter using platen compression. The combination of nanomechanical testing with nm/μN resolution and nano/micro fabrication leads to a particularly promising area. Here, uniform compression of thin film structures can be obtained for detailed ECM coupling experiments that are likely to help interpret current experimental results and build a systematic understanding of ECM effects across multiple scales.

We also note that while the general force and displacement ranges are shown in Figure 4A, and instrument accuracy is typically <1% of the full-scale measurement range, the achieved accuracy of an ECM coupling or other experiment highly depends on the sample design and the design of the experiment. For example, a nanoindenter may be able to achieve <1 nm displacement resolution under ideal conditions, but environmental vibrations and the sample quality may prevent a measurement from achieving such a displacement resolution with relevance for the intended property or process measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screw platforms with a force sensor & universal mechanical testers
ce:section.ce:para:
A common and direct setup for ECM studies is to place a solid-state cell under a uniaxial compressive force, using a load frame platform with a force sensor (which is easy to place in an inert atmosphere glove box) or a UMT (which is typically too large to easily place and operate in a glovebox). 14
,
24
,
72 Mechanical and electrochemical tests over days or longer can be carried out with these platforms, although thermal drift can affect the displacement readings.

First, considering the load frame platform, the structure mainly consists of three components, (1) a load frame with multiple screws and nuts to apply uniform vertical force to the test piece, (2) a test piece consisting of a coin-shaped cell, two or three electrical contacts (depending on if a reference electrode is included), and an electrically insulating sleeve, and (3) a force sensor. The pressure on the cell stack is calculated from the applied force and the sample area; the sample area should be checked post-test to account for possible expansion. With a sample area of 1 cm2 and a force sensor rated for kN, stack pressures of up to 10s of MPa can be achieved. As an example of a study with such a platform, Doux et al. observed the effect of stack pressure on the shorting behavior of lithium metal in Li6PS5Cl solid electrolyte. 14

The integration of battery testing with real-time stack pressure control and measurement has led to an improved understanding of degradation. For example, Lee and McDowell explained the different degradation process of Li10SnP2S12 and Li6PS5Cl (interface formation and dendrite growth, respectively) by tracking the relation of stack pressure with impedance measurements. 24 In another study, Zhang et al. constructed two similar batteries with different anodes (high volume expansion In/Li alloy and Li4Ti5O12 with negligible expansion) to study the expansion induced pressure and how it affects capacity. 73

Second, UMTs are widely used in bulk material mechanical testing such as obtaining stress-strain curves, elastic moduli, and fracture toughness as well as fatigue and creep parameters. This platform is analogous to screw platforms in its general working principles but with superior components for control and measurement of both force and displacement. Although generally large for a benchtop, there are examples of this platform used within an inert atmosphere glovebox. 15
,
19
,
49 Bulk lithium mechanical properties have been tested by Masias et al. and Le Page et al. 74 Ding et al. studied the creep deformation of lithium foil under cell-like conditions and noticed substantial creep and strain rate at pressures less than 2 MPa. 75 Wang et al. applied a UMT to study the effects of stack pressure on the Li/SE interface kinetics as well as the equilibrium potential. 19 In addition, a UMT may be used to measure interfacial adhesion, for example, with a peel test. 11
,
12 Adhesive strength can affect the interfacial delamination that can occur during lithium or delithiation.

The limitations of these platform were mentioned above: when applying force to particle-based components the local stresses are not uniform, and such platforms are also not suited to thin film samples because they have insufficient displacement resolution and control for <1 μm films. As for screw platforms, only applied force over a large area is monitored by force sensor, which hinders its application in smaller samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical deflectometry and other optical techniques
ce:section.ce:para: Optical deflectometry uses optical signals on a sample’s reflective surface to obtain its shape. This technique can be configured in a variety of ways, including a single beam scanning across a sample surface with measurement of the reflective beam spot position and observing the reflective fringe pattern of a collimated beam for local defect detection. In additional, one approach that has been particularly used for ECM studies is MOS, which uses multiple laser beams with stationary optics to obtain the sample surface curvature. 76 The multibeam optical sensor technique allows measurement of curvature in thin films (typically <1 μm) on mirror-flat substrates with well-defined mechanical properties (e.g., Si and SiO2), such that measuring wafer deflection allows the calculation of the strain/stress in the film on the wafer. This is a highly sensitive method that is able to detect substrate curvature radius changes on the kilometer scale. Because of its high sensitivity and compatibility with thin film samples, this technique is well suited for fundamental ECM studies for which an accurate state of stress or volume expansion is needed.

MOS on a wafer is a modified version of a cantilever beam experiment, which also uses the measurement of substrate deflection to calculate stresses within a film on the surface. The use of multiple beams reduces the effect of vibration and possible error introduced by single beam measurements. 77
,
78
,
79
,
80
,
81 The stress in the film is calculated from the wafer bend using an appropriate form of the Stoney equation (which is a standard relation between the wafer bend and the stress state in the sample) 82 or a model. An exemplar MOS experiment in the context of battery materials is the deposition of a thin film working electrode (e.g., 50–250 nm) of amorphous silicon on 200 nm of titanium on a 250 μm SiO2 wafer. 83 A porous separator, liquid electrolyte, and a Li metal counter electrode complete the cell. Such an experiment allows measurement of both compressive and tensile stresses in the film. This study showed that plastic deformation in the Si film occurred when the lithiation induced stress reached ∼1.2 GPa for a Si film thickness of 50 nm. In a related study Sethuraman et al. 71 measured a stress-potential coupling in Si thin films of 0.1–0.12 mV/MPa using relaxation experiments and attributed at least some of the widely observed hysteresis in cycling Si electrodes to this coupling. As another example, Nadimpalli et al. 84 used MOS along with electron microscopy to estimate the upper bound fracture resistance of lithiated amorphous Si to be 9–11 J/m2, and similar experiments with Na and amorphous germanium were conducted by Rakshit et al. 85 In a variant on this work with a liquid electrolyte, Cho et al. studied the cycling of Li metal electrodes (up to ∼30 μm) in a liquid electrolyte, and separated the stresses associated with lithium metal and SEI. 86 In addition to these examples involving a solid thin film and a liquid electrolyte, MOS can be applied to the study of structures more relevant to solid-state batteries. For example, Su et al. studied the coupled mechanical deformation and electrochemical behavior of a V2O5-x thin film electrode with a solid polymer electrolyte of varying molecular weights. 12 In another example, Cho et al. studied wafer bend and electrochemical behavior for a stacked structure including a quartz wafer, thin films of current collector, Li metal, and Al2O3, and an LLZO pellet. 87 They used mechanical modeling to relate the wafer bend to the electrochemical and physical processes taking place in this multi-layer structure during Li metal plating, while also noting the interpretive challenges and limitations of such structures and measurements for precise ECM coupling studies.

While MOS is a well-developed technique to characterize electrochemically induced stress in battery material, there are limitations. (1) The Stoney equation requires a sample thickness that is <<1% of the wafer thickness and full bonding among thin film layers and with the substrate. The elastic moduli of both the sample and the substrate must be well defined. Mechanical modeling is often used to overcome some of these limitations. (2) MOS requires a large substrate area to accommodate the multiple beams and results in only an average curvature over that area. This prohibits the measurement of local stress states and nonuniformities and is not useful for composite materials or patterned electrodes.

In addition to optical deflectometry, other optical techniques may also be used for measuring strain. For example, digital image correlation (DIC) may be used to capture the evolution of strain on a 2D surface by following the motion of speckles (e.g., graphite powder). The design of the optical system makes this approach suitable for a range of sampled areas, from the <1 μm to the mm or above. 74
,
88
,
89 When combined with a UMT or other load sensor, stress-strain relations may be directly measured, as has been done for bulk samples of Li metal within an Argon glove box. 74 Alternatively, DIC may be used to map the strain evolution in either the in-plane or through-plane surface of a solid-state cell, with per-pixel resolution of <1 μm. 88
,
89 2D maps of transient surface strain may then be directly correlated with the electrochemical behavior of the cell. As another example, 3D measurements of Li electrode morphology evolution during plating was measured using operando focus-variation microscopy, providing important transient insights on lithium electrodeposition. 90

The AFM is a scanning probe microscopy (SPM) technique with sub-nanometer resolution, as shown in Figures 4A and 4D, and involves a cantilever probe whose deflection is sensed with a laser and photodiode. A wide range of operating modes have been developed, including contact mode (applying force), conductive AFM (C-AFM), electrochemical strain microscopy (ESM), Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) to extract local potential, and electrochemical AFM (EC-AFM). 91 Hence, modes are available that can couple mechanics, local potential and current flow. The feedback control system enables working modes of constant height or constant force in the pN to nN range for purposes ranging from scanning surface topography to mechanical indentation. This probe-based method is useful for fundamental ECM surface studies, especially those related to transport of atoms, ions, and electrons, and interfacial kinetics.

To date there are two major configurations for AFM applications in electrochemistry experiments. First, the probe can be used to measure surface morphology and mechanical properties, without the probe passing current. For example, an electrochemical AFM mode (in which the probe is unbiased and only observes surface changes over time) has been used to monitor SEI formation, 92
,
93
,
94 mechanical properties of the SEI, volume changes in an active material during lithiation, 95 and the decreasing elastic modulus of silicon electrodes upon lithiation. 96 In addition to simply measuring surface morphology, a force-displacement curve may be measured at an array of points to extract a map of the elastic moduli.

Second, the probe itself may be configured to pass or sense concurrent with SPM. The electronic current measured by C-AFM reveals information on the chemical and physical properties of electrodes, for example, SEI formation (regions covered by SEI have lower conductance), 97 the lithium de-intercalation threshold voltage of LixCoO2, and phase transition behavior. C-AFM could also reveal local strain due to the strong coupling between strain and electronic conductivity for many insertion electrodes like LCO. 98 It has also been used in nanoscale thin film LiSSB systems to study reversible topographical changes, Li distribution, and pathways with enhanced diffusion. 99
,
100

KPFM has been used to map local changes in electric potential during lithium insertion/extraction simultaneously with local strain for LCO/LiPON/Si LiSSBs, revealing that the largest changes in strain and potential both occur at the SE/Si interface. 101 ESM, such as C-AFM, has been used to measure local surface strains associated with lithium transport as an electrical bias is applied while accounting for the local surface stiffness. This coupling of local strain and lithium concentration was shown in nanoscale mappings of amorphous silicon, 102 LCO, 103 LiFePO4, 104 and LiNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3
105 thin film electrodes.

AFM-derived scanning probe techniques, as with other methods, have four limitations. (1) The techniques are limited to solid surface analysis. For a liquid electrolyte/electrode interface, the AFM tip can pass through the liquid and interact directly with the electrode surface, whereas the solid electrolyte/electrode interface is typically “buried” and inaccessible. Addressing this limitation requires cross sectioning to expose the cell layers to the probe, after which the probe can sample the exposed interfaces of the cell. (2) The maximum scan area is about 100 μm × 100 μm, and the height range is <10 μm. (3) The extremely small sample volumes result in high sensitivity to local morphology and surface impurities, which can make quantitative and conclusive data interpretation challenging. (4) Perhaps the greatest challenge with scanning probe methods in general is their substantial sensitivity on the tip and sample surface condition, often resulting in lack of reproducibility. Recent advances in scanning probes, similar to those with conducting nanodiamonds, could help address such issues. 17

Nanoindentation was initially developed for indentation hardness testing of μm2 areas. Unlike a typical universal tester, a nanoindenter has a capacitive or piezo transducer that provides continuous measurement and/or control of force and displacement (typically to nN and nm resolution, respectively). The measurement produces a load-displacement curve from which elastic and plastic mechanical properties such as Young’s modulus, hardness, and fracture toughness can be extracted. 106 A scanning mode may also be used to obtain nm resolution surface topography maps. As in the case of AFM, numerous modes of nanoindentation have been developed, including conductive and electrochemical nanoindentation.

We emphasis that, as shown in Figure 4A, the nanoindentation platform is unique in its ability to use different force transducers and tips or platens to cover a wide range of applied loads and indent or compression patterns. For example, sharp tips such as Berkovich and cube corner have been used to characterize elastic responses and fracture toughness, whereas flat platens have been used for measuring polymeric and biological soft materials. 107
,
108 Thin-film deposition processes enable the fabrication of samples with areas matching that of a nanoindenter flat platen (i.e., hundreds of microns in diameter), which transforms the traditional indentation tests (with has a changing tip-sample contact area with tip displacement) to a compression test (with uniform platen-sample contact with displacements). Transducers with maximum forces of 10 N are available, which for an area of 100 μm × 100 μm under a flat platen would result in an applied stress of 1 GPa, which is relevant for solid-state battery studies as described previously. Because electrochemical reactions occur on an interface, the ability to apply a uniform pressure while measuring displacement is an important capability that tip-based indentation methods do not achieve. Further, while a typical nanoindentation force-displacement curve is measured in minutes, the instrument may also be used for applying and measuring force and displacement over days, which is important for measurements of creep and to study cycling behavior of solid-state structures. Finally, a nanoindenter is typically smaller than a universal tester, and may be placed more easily within an inert atmosphere glovebox for testing reactive battery materials.

Nanoindentation has been widely used for characterizing material mechanical behavior. Considering first mechanics-only measurements, it has been applied to a variety of battery materials. Nanoindentation has been applied to lithium foil 50 and deposited lithium films 109 as well as electrochemical interfaces with a liquid electrolyte. 110
,
111 The control system of a nanoindenter allows doing a matrix of nanoindentation tests on a sample surface to quantitatively map mechanical properties with greater statistical significance. By comparing a modulus map with measurements of the modulus vs. Li content of a material, the Li content can be obtained from a purely mechanical measurement of the electrode. 112 Measurement of the fracture properties of solid electrolyte materials has also been done with nanoindentation and is of critical importance for understanding and predicting dendrite propagation. 113
,
114 The nanoindenter is the most widely used instrument to measure fracture toughness of thin film structures.

As with other platforms, the use of nanoindentation has limitations. (1) The cost and complexity of use and interpretation are substantially higher than for screw platforms and universal testers, and the comparatively small volume of material sampled requires careful sample and experiment design. (2) Integration with an inert atmosphere glovebox and an electrical tester is an additional barrier to use for battery material and battery cell testing. (3) When using a nanoindenter with a flat platen and a stiff, solid-state sample, achieving a uniform compressive stress over the sample area requires careful alignment between the platen and the sample. The contact area is likely limited to <1 mm2 and requires special methods to achieve alignment.

Solid-state Li metal batteries have great technical and commercial potential but require an expanded engagement with solid mechanics measurement and theory development. While electrochemistry research groups can obtain and use simple compression fixtures for solid-state cell fabrication and performance testing, there are critical gaps in measuring the important elastic and plastic behaviors of leading LiSSB materials, as well as creating platforms that allow for high-accuracy, fundamental measurements of electrochemical-mechanical couplings in the areas of thermodynamics, transport, and kinetics.

In terms of mechanical property measurements, there are particular measurement gaps of plastic behaviors (including creep and fracture behavior) as a function of sample volume, mechanical boundary conditions, and temperature, including for Li metal and leading solid electrolytes. In terms of fundamental measurements of ECM couplings, there are particular gaps in measurement of coupling between stress/strain and transport (ionic, electronic, and diffusion), and also stress/strain and interfacial kinetics.

Here, we have covered the major classes of efforts in these areas at present and presented an overview of the major mechanical testing platforms and their advantages and limitations. We have highlighted the transition between sharp tip indentation methods at scales of μm and below, and flat platen compression methods at larger length scales. We wish to again emphasize particular opportunities in the use of nanoindentation and thin film structures (especially lateral cell structures) for fundamental ECM studies. Experiments designed with uniform test structures with controlled geometry combined with a way to apply localized or distributed forces using controlled nanoindentation can answer fundamental questions related to ECM coupling on interfacial thermodynamics as well as how stress affects material properties and cell performance.

It is crucial to ensure that the force applied by the nanoindenter transducer gets transferred to the surface and interfaces of thin film cells uniformly. Surface contact mechanics play a critical role, and the force transferred depends on the alignment, surface roughness, aspect ratio, and Young’s modulus of the indenter tip (platen), and on the thickness of the intermediate cell layers. Despite these challenges, we believe that there are exciting opportunities to develop new measurement methods and theory to advance the fundamental understanding and practical development of solid-state battery science, materials, and cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Y.S., B.B., D.M.S., and P.A. conceived of the content and wrote the paper. G.W.R. and A.A.T. reviewed and edited the final version of the paper.
P.A. has consulted for solid-state battery investors and companies, and holds several patents related to solid-state batteries.
